children.text: Any agreement entered into under section 2902(b) or (c) of this title shall enter into force with respect to the United States if (and only if)—

the President, at least 90 calendar days before the day on which he enters into the trade agreement, notifies the House of Representatives and the Senate of his intention to enter into the agreement, and promptly thereafter publishes notice of such intention in the Federal Register;
after entering into the agreement, the President submits a document to the House of Representatives and to the Senate containing a copy of the final legal text of the agreement, together with—
a draft of an implementing bill,
a statement of any administrative action proposed to implement the trade agreement, and
the supporting information described in paragraph (2); and
the implementing bill is enacted into law.
The supporting information required under paragraph (1)(B)(iii) consists of—
an explanation as to how the implementing bill and proposed administrative action will change or affect existing law; and
asserting that the agreement makes progress in achieving the applicable purposes, policies, and objectives of this title,
setting forth the reasons of the President regarding—

how and to what extent the agreement makes progress in achieving the applicable purposes, policies, and objectives referred to in clause (i), and why and to what extent the agreement does not achieve other applicable purposes, policies, and objectives,
how the agreement serves the interests of United States commerce, and
why the implementing bill and proposed administrative action is required or appropriate to carry out the agreement;
describing the efforts made by the President to obtain international exchange rate equilibrium and any effect the agreement may have regarding increased international monetary stability; and
describing the extent, if any, to which—

each foreign country that is a party to the agreement maintains non-commercial state trading enterprises that may adversely affect, nullify, or impair the benefits to the United States under the agreement, and
the agreement applies to or affects purchases and sales by such enterprises.

To ensure that a foreign country which receives benefits under a trade agreement entered into under section 2902(b) or (c) of this title is subject to the obligations imposed by such agreement, the President shall recommend to Congress in the implementing bill and statement of administrative action submitted with respect to such agreement that the benefits and obligations of such agreement apply solely to the parties to such agreement, if such application is consistent with the terms of such agreement. The President may also recommend with respect to any such agreement that the benefits and obligations of such agreement not apply uniformly to all parties to such agreement, if such application is consistent with the terms of such agreement.
Application of Congressional “fast track” procedures to implementing bills

children.tail: (hereinafter in this section referred to as “fast track procedures”) apply to implementing bills submitted with respect to trade agreements entered into under section 2902(b) or (c) of this title before

children.text: such fast track procedures shall be extended to implementing bills submitted with respect to trade agreements entered into under section 2902(b) or (c) of this title after
the President requests such extension under paragraph (2); and
neither House of the Congress adopts an extension disapproval resolution under paragraph (5) before
If the President is of the opinion that the fast track procedures should be extended to implementing bills described in paragraph (1)(B), the President must submit to the Congress, no later than

children.tail: , a written report that contains a request for such extension, together with—

children.text: a description of all trade agreements that have been negotiated under section 2902(b) or (c) of this title and the anticipated schedule for submitting such agreements to the Congress for approval;

a description of the progress that has been made in multilateral and bilateral negotiations to achieve the purposes, policies, and objectives of this title, and a statement that such progress justifies the continuation of negotiations; and
a statement of the reasons why the extension is needed to complete the negotiations.
The President shall promptly inform the Advisory Committee for Trade Policy and Negotiations established under

children.tail: of his decision to submit a report to Congress under paragraph (2). The Advisory Committee shall submit to the Congress as soon as practicable, but no later than

children.text: its views regarding the progress that has been made in multilateral and bilateral negotiations to achieve the purposes, policies, and objectives of this title; and
a statement of its views, and the reasons therefor, regarding whether the extension requested under paragraph (2) should be approved or disapproved.
The reports submitted to the Congress under paragraphs (2) and (3), or any portion of the reports, may be classified to the extent the President determines appropriate.

For purposes of this subsection, the term “extension disapproval resolution” means a resolution of either House of the Congress, the sole matter after the resolving clause of which is as follows: “That the            disapproves the request of the President for the extension, under section 1103(b)(1)(B)(i) of the Omnibus Trade and Competitiveness Act of 1988, of the provisions of section 151 of the Trade Act of 1974 to any implementing bill submitted with respect to any trade agreement entered into under section 1102(b) or (c) of such Act after

children.tail: , because sufficient tangible progress has not been made in trade negotiations.”, with the blank space being filled with the name of the resolving House of the Congress.

children.text: may be introduced in either House of the Congress by any member of such House; and
shall be jointly referred, in the House of Representatives, to the Committee on Ways and Means and the Committee on Rules.
The provisions of section 2192(d) and (e) of this title (relating to the floor consideration of certain resolutions in the House and Senate) apply to extension disapproval resolutions.
It is not in order for—
the Senate to consider any extension disapproval resolution not reported by the Committee on Finance;
the House of Representatives to consider any extension disapproval resolution not reported by the Committee on Ways and Means and the Committee on Rules; or
either House of the Congress to consider an extension disapproval resolution that is reported to such House after
Limitations on use of “fast track” procedures

The fast track procedures shall not apply to any implementing bill submitted with respect to a trade agreement entered into under section 2902(b) or (c) of this title if both Houses of the Congress separately agree to procedural disapproval resolutions within any 60-day period.
shall be introduced by the chairman or ranking minority member of the Committee on Ways and Means or the chairman or ranking minority member of the Committee on Rules,
shall be jointly referred to the Committee on Ways and Means and the Committee on Rules, and
may not be amended by either Committee; and
in the Senate shall be original resolutions of the Committee on Finance.
The provisions of section 2192(d) and (e) of this title (relating to the floor consideration of certain resolutions in the House and Senate) apply to procedural disapproval resolutions.
It is not in order for the House of Representatives to consider any procedural disapproval resolution not reported by the Committee on Ways and Means and the Committee on Rules.

For purposes of this subsection, the term “procedural disapproval resolution” means a resolution of either House of the Congress, the sole matter after the resolving clause of which is as follows: “That the President has failed or refused to consult with Congress on trade negotiations and trade agreements in accordance with the provisions of the Omnibus Trade and Competitiveness Act of 1988, and, therefore, the provisions of section 151 of the Trade Act of 1974 shall not apply to any implementing bill submitted with respect to any trade agreement entered into under section 1102(b) or (c) of such Act of 1988, if, during the 60-day period beginning on the date on which this resolution is agreed to by the           , the            agrees to a procedural disapproval resolution (within the meaning of section 1103(c)(1)(E) of such Act of 1988).”, with the first blank space being filled with the name of the resolving House of the Congress and the second blank space being filled with the name of the other House of the Congress.
The fast track procedures shall not apply to any implementing bill that contains a provision approving of any trade agreement which is entered into under

children.tail: with any foreign country if either—
are not met with respect to the negotiation of such agreement; or

children.text: the Committee on Finance of the Senate or the Committee on Ways and Means of the House of Representatives disapproves of the negotiation of such agreement before the close of the 60-day period which begins on the date notice is provided under

children.tail: with respect to the negotiation of such agreement.

children.text: Rules of House of Representatives and Senate
Subsections (b) and (c) are enacted by the Congress—

as an exercise of the rulemaking power of the House of Representatives and the Senate, respectively, and as such is deemed a part of the rules of each House, respectively, and such procedures supersede other rules only to the extent that they are inconsistent with such other rules; and

with the full recognition of the constitutional right of either House to change the rules (so far as relating to the procedures of that House) at any time, in the same manner, and to the same extent as any other rule of that House.
Computation of certain periods of time
Each period of time described in subsection (c)(1)(A) and (E) and (2) of this section shall be computed without regard to—
the days on which either House of Congress is not in session because of an adjournment of more than 3 days to a day certain or an adjournment of the Congress sine die; and
any Saturday and Sunday, not excluded under paragraph (1), when either House of the Congress is not in session.
This title, referred to in subsecs. (a)(2)(B)(i) and (b)(2)(B), (3)(A), is title I (§ 1001 et seq.) of
